summary:
The ideal range should be low enough to minimize health hazards from excess mercury but still provide adequate restoration strength; thus, a level close to or slightly above minimum standards like those suggested would likely offer both durability and reduced risk. Agent1 concluded that an appropriate choice was option B due to its alignment with established safety protocols concerning minimized toxicity without jeopardizing structural adequacy needed clinically. 

The summary captures essential aspects regarding balancing performance needs versus stringent adherence to modern safety measures related specifically back to what percentage represents acceptable yet protective residue amounts post-restoration placement procedures involving silver alloy materials commonly used today globally among dentists trained under similar curricula emphasizing public welfare through informed decision-making processes guided scientifically validated research findings reported over decades now informing professional consensus views widely accepted worldwide including here locally too!
